div.div: Effect: A handkerchief is tied into what looks like an impossibly complex knotted ball. You place it behind your back — and a moment later reveal it completely untied. Method: fold the handkerchief into thirds lengthwise. Tie it into an overhand knot, but layer the corners in a specific mirrored sequence as you tie — this creates a 'knot' that looks solid but reverses easily. Behind your back, simply pull the layered corners in reverse order. The 'knot' falls apart instantly. Bring the handkerchief out spread flat with a flourish.

The mythological Gordian Knot (cut by Alexander the Great in 333 BC) was so culturally prominent that Victorian magicians regularly borrowed its name for complicated-looking but secretly simple knot effects, adding classical gravitas to their performances.

A handkerchief is tied into what appears to be an impossibly tight, complex ball. The magician places it behind their back and — a moment later — reveals it completely untied. The secret: the corners are layered in a specific way that reverses the process when pulled correctly.

svg.text: Source: The Magician's Own Book (1862) · Heritage Parlor
